transcript:
Here's what you're looking at: sixty words — Japanese, Arabic, Greek, Hindi, Russian, dozens more — drifting in slow orbits around a dark blue void, each one perfectly formed, none of them settling anywhere.

Look at the group name in the code: the words all live inside an element literally called "tongues without mothers." And the words themselves aren't random vocabulary — the artist chose them in clusters: "word" in a dozen languages, then "memory," then "dream," then "home." So the piece is quietly asking the same question in every tongue it knows, circling the concept of belonging without ever landing on it. Even the animation function is named "breathe without lungs" — motion that mimics life without the thing that would make it necessary.

What gets me is the rotation line — each word is turned to always face outward, "always addressing, never resting," the comment says. These words aren't just orbiting a center. They're perpetually speaking to someone who isn't there.
